course_title: Contemporary Theories of Film and Television
course_description: MSTU3150 is a course that explores recent approaches to the study of motion pictures as art. The course is designed to draw upon and build upon the gateway and cornerstone courses, as well as complement the capstone course, of the Film and Television major. We will explore the particular, formal qualities of motion pictures as well as the meanings and values of particular movements and works in conjunction with some recent publications from a broadly aesthetic perspective. The central aim of the course is to develop your capacity to make judgements about the films or television shows you watch; judgement, as we will discover, is central to the act of critical writing about them.

course_aims: This course aims to familiarise students with recent approaches, developed both within film/tv studies and philosophy of art, to the study of motion pictures as art. As such, one goal is to provide students with an understanding of film/tv studies as a discipline, its relation to scholarship in other disciplines. More specifically, the course strives to facilitate students' critical thinking and abstract reasoning about the nature and value of motion pictures. It also endeavors to bolster transferable critical thinking and communication skills via intensive discussion sessions and writing.

learning_outcomes.description: Understand the development and relationship of distinct disciplinary approaches to studying motion pictures as art.
Use the terms, concepts, and arguments of a particular scholarly approach to develop original claims and analyses.
Critically evaluate pieces of scholarly writing to demonstrate understanding of different perspectives and approaches and their potential merits, constraints, and flaws.
Demonstrate understanding of the fundamental attributes of and issues related to aesthetic criticism and evaluation and apply such understanding to set films.
Demonstrate capacity to produce original criticism and develop one’s own critical voice.

assessment_details.title: Writing Evaluation
assessment_details.task_description: The purpose of this piece of assessment is to have you weigh the merits, constraints, and flaws of a piece of academic writing about one of the set screenings from the first four weeks of class. You are being asked to produce an essayistic annotation of one of the case study readings that is in some way related to one of the set screenings. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submit via TurnItIn through Blackboard.
TurnItIn Receipts:
Assignments for this course will be submitted electronically via Blackboard and using TurnItIn . Before submitting any assignments for this course you must ensure you have completed UQ's compulsory online Academic Integrity Tutorial.
When you successfully submit your assessment to TurnItIn you will see text confirming your submission is complete, before being redirected to your Assignment inbox. On this page you can:
View the name of the submitted file View date and time of the upload Resubmit your paper (if necessary) Download your submitted paper Download digital receipt.
If you cannot see your submission in your Assignment inbox you should regard your submission as unsuccessful. Students are responsible for retaining evidence of submission by the due date for all assessment items, in the required form (e.g. screenshot, email, photo, and an unaltered copy of submitted work).
If the submission was not successful:
Note the error message (preferably take a screenshot). Go to your assignment page and see if it is possible to submit again. If you cannot submit again email your course coordinator immediately with the assignment attached.
Please visit this webpage for further advice on how to submit your TurnItIn assignment .
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.task_description: You are required to write a piece of criticism that demonstrates your strengths in the evaluation of film. Some essay suggestions will be released mid-way through the semester, and you may wish to consult with the course coordinator about designing your own essay topic. assessment_details.title: In-class writing exercises
assessment_details.task_description: The aim of the writing tasks is to give you frequent opportunities to practice writing analyses that weave together objective features of the week’s set screenings with a particular point or claim you would like to make about them. The questions will differ week-to-week but assess the same set of skills. This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_activities.topic: WEEK ONE: Aesthetic Criticism This week we will explore the following questions: What is aesthetic criticism? What is the value of criticism? How do we try to make objective claims about artworks and their aesthetic value? What is the role of judgment? What do we focus on when we “do” aesthetic criticism? What weight do aesthetic judgements and evaluations carry (why might they matter)? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
WEEK ONE: NO TUTORIALS

WEEK TWO: Subjectivity, Preference, Taste This week we will explore the following questions: How do claims about the objective qualities of art involve the subjectivity of the critic? What is the role of selection? Are the so-called subjective and objective opposed, or do they inform each other? How do we choose the “right” language to write about art, and what does that say about us as individual critics? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

WEEK TWO: Subjectivity, Preference, Taste This week we will explore the following questions: How do claims about the objective qualities of art involve the subjectivity of the critic? What is the role of selection? Are the so-called subjective and objective opposed, or do they inform each other? How do we choose the “right” language to write about art, and what does that say about us as individual critics? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05

WEEK THREE: Obstacles in Language and Criticism This week we will explore the following questions: How do we meet the challenge of translating experiential, audio-visual, tactile art into language? What is it we are actually trying to describe in aesthetic criticism? How is it that we can use language to conjure the experience of cinema? In what way does aesthetic criticism’s translation of experience work to cohere together the objective and subjective dimensions of our engagement with art? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

WEEK THREE: Obstacles in Language and Criticism This week we will explore the following questions: How do we meet the challenge of translating experiential, audio-visual, tactile art into language? What is it we are actually trying to describe in aesthetic criticism? How is it that we can use language to conjure the experience of cinema? In what way does aesthetic criticism’s translation of experience work to cohere together the objective and subjective dimensions of our engagement with art? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05

WEEK FOUR: Badness and Negative Evaluations This week we will explore the following questions: How is that we can make claims about the aesthetic flaws of artworks? How is it that we develop and justify the critical criteria against which we evaluate works of art? Why do we value the values that inform our aesthetic judgements about art? How do different clusters of critical criteria illustrate the subjective dimensions of our experience and engagement with art? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

WEEK FOUR: Badness and Negative Evaluations This week we will explore the following questions: How is that we can make claims about the aesthetic flaws of artworks? How is it that we develop and justify the critical criteria against which we evaluate works of art? Why do we value the values that inform our aesthetic judgements about art? How do different clusters of critical criteria illustrate the subjective dimensions of our experience and engagement with art? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05
WEEK FIVE: READING WEEK NO LECTURE
WEEK FIVE: READING WEEK NO TUTORIALS

WEEK SIX: Beauty This week we will explore the following questions: What is beauty in the context of art? Why do we value beauty? Are there ways in which art can complicate our notions of beauty, and if so, how? How does beauty as a concept work to invoke other aesthetic qualities? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

WEEK SIX: Beauty This week we will explore the following questions: What is beauty in the context of art? Why do we value beauty? Are there ways in which art can complicate our notions of beauty, and if so, how? How does beauty as a concept work to invoke other aesthetic qualities? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05

WEEK SEVEN: Mood and Atmosphere This week we will explore the following questions: What is mood or atmosphere in the context of art? How is it that films can be said to have a mood or atmosphere? In what ways do we identify mood/atmosphere in particular films? How do we articulate this in language, and why might they be aesthetically valuable aesthetic qualities? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

WEEK SEVEN: Mood and Atmosphere This week we will explore the following questions: What is mood or atmosphere in the context of art? How is it that films can be said to have a mood or atmosphere? In what ways do we identify mood/atmosphere in particular films? How do we articulate this in language, and why might they be aesthetically valuable aesthetic qualities? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05

WEEK EIGHT: Tactility and Feeling This week we will explore the following questions: What is the difference between mood/atmosphere and tactility/feeling? What is it for films to have a particular tactile quality or feeling? How can films evoke tactile responses in spectators? In what ways can films deliberately amplify the artform’s concern with textures and surfaces, and the felt qualities of experience? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

WEEK EIGHT: Tactility and Feeling This week we will explore the following questions: What is the difference between mood/atmosphere and tactility/feeling? What is it for films to have a particular tactile quality or feeling? How can films evoke tactile responses in spectators? In what ways can films deliberately amplify the artform’s concern with textures and surfaces, and the felt qualities of experience? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05

WEEK NINE: Rhythm and Pacing This week we will explore the following questions: In what ways do we identify the stylistic and formal underpinnings of a film’s rhythm and pacing? What is it for a film to have “good” rhythm and “good” pacing? How does this flow into other attributes and aesthetic qualities of a particular film? How do we make an evaluative case for rhythm and pacing? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

WEEK NINE: Rhythm and Pacing This week we will explore the following questions: In what ways do we identify the stylistic and formal underpinnings of a film’s rhythm and pacing? What is it for a film to have “good” rhythm and “good” pacing? How does this flow into other attributes and aesthetic qualities of a particular film? How do we make an evaluative case for rhythm and pacing? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05
Mid-Semester Break
WEEK TEN: NO LECTURE
WEEK TEN: NO TUTORIALS

WEEK ELEVEN: Metacinema and Intermediality GUEST LECTURE: Keya Makar This week we will explore the following questions: How does the self-reference and reflexivity of films play into their aesthetic value? Likewise, how can a film’s deliberate reference to and invocation of other artforms complicate and expand the means by which we make evaluative judgments about their aesthetic value? To what extent does this uniquely rely on spectator knowledge of different frames of reference? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

WEEK ELEVEN: Metacinema and Intermediality This week we will explore the following questions: How does the self-reference and reflexivity of films play into their aesthetic value? Likewise, how can a film’s deliberate reference to and invocation of other artforms complicate and expand the means by which we make evaluative judgments about their aesthetic value? To what extent does this uniquely rely on spectator knowledge of different frames of reference? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05

WEEK TWELVE: Violence and the Grotesque: Southern Gothic Cinema This week we will explore the following questions: In what ways does the depiction of violence problematise our evaluative judgements? In what ways are ethical issues related to the representation of violence a factor that requires our critical attention? How expansive is violence as a concept in the ways in which it is examined in art? How do we evaluate particular instances of broader artistic categories? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

WEEK TWELVE: Violence and the Grotesque: Southern Gothic Cinema This week we will explore the following questions: In what ways does the depiction of violence problematise our evaluative judgements? In what ways are ethical issues related to the representation of violence a factor that requires our critical attention? How expansive is violence as a concept in the ways in which it is examined in art? How do we evaluate particular instances of broader artistic categories? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05

WEEK THIRTEEN: Feminist Aesthetics GUEST LECTURE: Dr Bonnie Evans This week we will explore the following questions: In what ways can theoretical perspectives such as feminism complicate aesthetic criticism? Do such perspectives undermine or undercut the interests of aesthetic criticism? Can they be fruitfully combined? Do theoretical perspectives enable a productive expansion of the interests and values of aesthetic criticism? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

WEEK THIRTEEN: Feminist Aesthetics This week we will explore the following questions: In what ways can theoretical perspectives such as feminism complicate aesthetic criticism? Do such perspectives undermine or undercut the interests of aesthetic criticism? Can they be fruitfully combined? Do theoretical perspectives enable a productive expansion of the interests and values of aesthetic criticism? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library . Course guidelines Communication Expectations While you are a student at UQ, all communication must be conducted according to the UQ Student Code of Conduct . The UQ Library has a helpful Communicate and collaborate online module . Email is the primary way for you to send messages to, and receive information from, the School and our staff. You must use your UQ email address (not a private address) to communicate with staff. You should add a clear subject line, including course code, and a 2-3 word statement. You can send email at any time, however, please do not expect responses outside normal working hours (Monday to Friday from ~8am to ~5pm). Emails that constitute bullying, harassment or discrimination against staff contravene the Student Code of Conduct. Emails like this will be reported to the University, and the matter will be pursued as misconduct. Learning activities
